findings.title: Secrets stored as plaintext in Helm values files
findings.description: MinIO access keys (minio.accessKeyId, minio.secretAccessKey), Redis auth passwords (redis.auth.password), and CNPG passwords (cnpg.password) are passed via Helm values files (values-staging.yaml, values-production.yaml). These files are typically committed to version control, meaning credentials would be stored in plaintext in the repository. The plan acknowledges native Secrets limitations and defers ESO, but does not address how values files containing secrets are protected during the interim.
findings.recommendation: Never commit credential values to values files in version control. Use Helm's --set or --set-file flags to inject secrets at deploy time from a secure source (e.g., CI/CD pipeline variables, Vault, or sealed-secrets). Add values-staging.yaml and values-production.yaml to .gitignore or use a values-secrets.yaml file that is explicitly excluded. Provide a values-secrets.yaml.example template with placeholder values. Consider adopting Sealed Secrets or SOPS-encrypted values as a near-term alternative before full ESO migration.

findings.title: No TLS encryption for inter-service database and message broker connections
findings.description: The expanded tasks deploy Postgres (CNPG), Redis, and NATS without any mention of TLS for client-to-server or intra-cluster connections. Connection strings in Secrets and ConfigMaps use plaintext protocols (redis://, nats://, postgres:// without sslmode). While CiliumNetworkPolicies restrict network access, traffic within the namespace traverses the pod network unencrypted, exposing credentials and data to potential eavesdropping by any compromised pod or node-level attacker.
findings.recommendation: Enable TLS for all backing services: (1) CNPG supports TLS natively — set spec.postgresql.pg_hba entries to require SSL and configure server certificates. (2) Redis bitnami chart supports TLS via redis.tls.enabled=true. (3) NATS supports TLS via nats.config.tls. Update connection strings to use TLS variants (rediss://, nats+tls://, sslmode=require for Postgres). For v1, at minimum enable TLS for Postgres connections carrying credentials; add Redis and NATS TLS as a fast-follow.

findings.title: No secret rotation mechanism or plan documented
findings.description: The plan explicitly acknowledges that native Kubernetes Secrets do not auto-rotate and defers ESO migration. However, there is no interim rotation procedure documented — no runbook for rotating MinIO access keys, Redis passwords, or database credentials. Long-lived static credentials increase the window of exposure if any credential is compromised.
findings.recommendation: Document a manual secret rotation runbook in the README (task 1013) with step-by-step procedures for each credential type. Include: (1) Generate new credentials, (2) Update Helm values or Kubernetes Secrets, (3) Rolling restart dependent workloads, (4) Revoke old credentials. Set a calendar reminder for quarterly rotation. Track ESO migration as a prioritized follow-up with a target date.

findings.title: MinIO root credentials used in bucket setup Job without scoped access
findings.description: The MinIO capacity check script (1004) and bucket setup Job (1005) require MinIO root user credentials to create buckets, users, and policies. These root credentials are mounted into Job pods. If the Job pod spec is retained or logs are captured, root credentials could leak. Additionally, the root credential Secret must pre-exist but there's no specification for how it's created or protected.
findings.recommendation: Create a dedicated MinIO admin user with only the permissions needed for bucket and user management (mc admin policy) rather than using root credentials. Ensure the Job's serviceAccountName has minimal RBAC. Add explicit log scrubbing (avoid echoing credentials in Job scripts). Set the Job's ttlSecondsAfterFinished to auto-clean completed Job pods. Document the pre-existing root Secret creation as a prerequisite with restricted access.

findings.title: Container images in Helm test pods and Jobs use mutable or latest tags
findings.description: Test pods and Job manifests reference images with mutable tags: 'postgres:16-alpine', 'redis:7-alpine', 'natsio/nats-box:latest', 'minio/mc:latest', 'busybox:latest'. Mutable tags can resolve to different image digests over time, introducing supply chain risk if an upstream image is compromised or introduces breaking changes.
findings.recommendation: Pin all container image references to specific digests (sha256) or at minimum to specific patch version tags (e.g., postgres:16.4-alpine, redis:7.2.5-alpine, minio/mc:RELEASE.2024-xx-xx). Add image pull policy: IfNotPresent. Consider using a private registry mirror to cache and scan images before use.

findings.title: No pod security standards or security contexts defined
findings.description: None of the task specifications mention Kubernetes Pod Security Standards (PSS), SecurityContexts, or PodSecurityAdmission policies. Without these, pods may run as root, with writable root filesystems, or with elevated privileges. This applies to all deployed workloads, test pods, and Jobs.
findings.recommendation: Apply Pod Security Standards at the namespace level using PodSecurityAdmission labels (e.g., pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted). In all pod specs, set securityContext with: runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true (where possible), allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, drop all capabilities. Configure these as defaults in the Helm values and apply to subchart pods via subchart securityContext overrides.

findings.title: No security event logging or audit trail for infrastructure operations
findings.description: The plan includes no tasks for logging security-relevant events: Secret access, RBAC denials, network policy violations, MinIO access logs, database authentication failures, or administrative operations. The scale tasks include observability (OpenTelemetry, Jaeger, alerting) but focus on SLO metrics, not security events.
findings.recommendation: Add audit logging configuration: (1) Enable MinIO server access logging and audit logging to a dedicated log stream. (2) Enable CNPG Postgres log_connections, log_disconnections, and log_statement for DDL. (3) Configure Cilium to export network policy drop events (Hubble). (4) In scale task 1024, add alert rules for: repeated auth failures on Postgres/Redis, network policy denials, and Secret access anomalies via Kubernetes audit logs. (5) Ship all security-relevant logs to a centralized logging system.

findings.title: Service account Role grants create/delete on Jobs without scope limitation
findings.description: The hermes-pipeline-sa Role (task 1002) grants create/delete verbs on Jobs. While this may be needed for pipeline operations, it allows any workload using this service account to create arbitrary Jobs in the namespace, which could be used for privilege escalation or resource abuse if a workload is compromised.
findings.recommendation: Review whether create/delete on Jobs is strictly necessary for runtime workloads. If Jobs are only created by CI/CD or Helm hooks, use a separate service account for those operations. If the pipeline SA must create Jobs, add a validating admission webhook or OPA/Gatekeeper constraint that restricts Job specs (e.g., only allowed images, resource limits enforced).

findings.title: No dependency auditing or vulnerability scanning tasks
findings.description: There are no tasks for auditing Helm chart dependencies (bitnami/redis, nats/nats, minio/minio), scanning container images for CVEs, or verifying chart provenance. The subcharts are referenced by name but versions are not pinned in the task descriptions.
findings.recommendation: Pin exact versions for all Helm subchart dependencies in Chart.yaml (e.g., bitnami/redis version 19.x.x, nats/nats version 1.x.x). Add a CI pipeline step that runs 'helm dependency audit' or equivalent. Integrate Trivy or Grype container image scanning as a pre-deployment gate. Verify chart signatures if available from the chart repository.

findings.title: MinIO presigned URLs have configurable but potentially long expiry
findings.description: MINIO_PRESIGN_EXPIRY defaults to 3600 seconds (1 hour). While configurable, the plan does not specify guidance on minimum/maximum acceptable values. Long-lived presigned URLs can be shared or leaked, granting unauthorized access to artifacts.
findings.recommendation: Document security guidance for presigned URL expiry in the README. Recommend production default of 300-900 seconds. Add Helm values validation (e.g., max 3600s) via a Helm template assertion. Consider implementing server-side access logging for presigned URL usage to detect anomalous access patterns.

findings.title: Cross-namespace isolation relies solely on CiliumNetworkPolicies
findings.description: Isolation between hermes-staging and hermes-production namespaces is enforced via CiliumNetworkPolicies (task 1003). While RBAC prevents service account cross-access (task 1002), if Cilium is misconfigured, disabled, or bypassed (e.g., host networking), the network isolation breaks. The plan correctly tests this but has no defense-in-depth.
findings.recommendation: This is already well-handled with the planned test (cross-namespace curl). As defense-in-depth, ensure Cilium is configured in strict mode (no fall-through), add a Kubernetes-native NetworkPolicy as a secondary layer (Cilium respects both), and consider deploying an admission controller that prevents pods from using hostNetwork: true in Hermes namespaces.

findings.title: Scale tasks lack TLS for ingress and inter-cluster communication
findings.description: Scale task 1018 (cert-manager TLS for ingress) and 1019 (ingress controller with TLS termination) are correctly planned but are marked as depending on ALL base tasks completing first. Until these scale tasks execute, any externally-exposed endpoint operates without TLS. Additionally, there is no mention of mTLS for inter-service communication within the mesh.
findings.recommendation: Prioritize tasks 1018 and 1019 to execute immediately after base infrastructure is stable, before any external traffic is routed. Consider whether any services are externally exposed during the base task phase — if so, add a temporary self-signed TLS configuration. For inter-service mTLS, evaluate Cilium's mutual authentication feature or a service mesh sidecar if strict zero-trust is required.

strengths: Excellent namespace isolation design with default-deny CiliumNetworkPolicies, explicit cross-namespace blocking, and targeted egress rules for MinIO and DNS.
RBAC follows least-privilege principles with namespace-scoped Roles (not ClusterRoleBindings) and dedicated service accounts per namespace.
Clear separation of secrets from ConfigMaps — the plan explicitly mandates no credentials in ConfigMaps and includes test verification for this.
ResourceQuotas and LimitRanges are well-defined per environment, preventing noisy-neighbor and resource exhaustion attacks.
MinIO bucket policy isolation (per-environment access keys scoped to specific buckets) prevents cross-environment data access.
Comprehensive test strategy including RBAC verification, network policy enforcement testing, and secret/configmap completeness checks.
PodDisruptionBudgets for production backing services ensure availability during maintenance operations.
The capacity gate check for MinIO (task 1004) demonstrates thoughtful blast radius management for shared infrastructure.
summary: The infrastructure plan demonstrates strong foundational security with proper namespace isolation, least-privilege RBAC, and network policies. However, there are two high-severity gaps that should be addressed before production deployment: secrets in Helm values files risk credential exposure in version control, and the absence of TLS for database/broker connections leaves inter-service traffic unencrypted. Medium-severity findings around pod security standards, secret rotation procedures, and security audit logging should be addressed as fast-follow items. The plan's overall architecture is sound, and addressing these findings will bring it to a production-hardened posture.
